sutras.text: Your ADHD brain is a creative superpower that enables hyperfocus on design work, but you systematically avoid administrative tasks that could transform your business—the revision policy you've started writing six times is more important than the next Blender tutorial.
You get sidetracked by perfectionist rabbit holes (font comparisons in Figma, donut iterations in Blender) when the real creative breakthrough comes from shipping incomplete work to real clients.

You've achieved the freelance milestone of $2,200+ monthly recurring income and dropped the barista job, yet you still only pay the $116 minimum on your credit card—your income problem is solved, your spending psychology isn't.

You spend consistently on premium groceries at Whole Foods and daily $18 transportation while expressing anxiety about a $6k debt that your current income could eliminate in 6 months with basic budgeting.

You've successfully raised your hourly rate and clients who left over price weren't worth keeping, but you still undercharge on time-intensive projects because you haven't completed the revision policy that would prevent scope creep.
Your portfolio hit 1,000 unique visitors from a single Instagram post, yet you struggle with networking anxiety at design meetups—your work speaks louder than you think it does.

You've questioned staying in Austin due to rising costs at least six times over two years, but your social life, creative community, and client base are all rooted there—the cost anxiety is masking a deeper fear about committing to your chosen path.

You have five major unfinished business items that would each unlock significant progress: the revision policy (client boundaries), tax forms (financial clarity), Blender course completion (3D skills), credit card payoff plan (debt freedom), and business development follow-ups (revenue growth).

You consistently start learning projects (Blender donut, motion design, 3D tutorials) but abandon them before completion, while your actual client work gets finished—the difference is external accountability versus self-direction.

top_insights: Your financial stress isn't about income (you've solved that)—it's about spending psychology and avoiding administrative tasks like budget planning
The revision policy you keep starting but never finishing is the single biggest lever for transforming your freelance business from reactive to structured
Your ADHD creative superpowers need administrative scaffolding to be sustainable—embrace boring systems to protect your creative flow

sutras.data_story: Theo achieved his freelance milestone of sustainable income and dropped part-time work, but paradoxically still struggles with financial anxiety due to avoidance of administrative tasks and spending psychology. His creative work thrives (1k portfolio visitors, rate increases, steady $2,200+ monthly income) while basic business infrastructure remains incomplete, creating a cycle where ADHD-driven creative hyperfocus succeeds but executive function gaps limit systematic growth.

timeline_entries.text: USER: I undercharged a client again. $600 for a brand identity that took 40 hours. How do I stop doing this? ASSISTANT: Pricing anxiety is almost always the root cause. The fix isn't a rate card — it's building the muscle of saying numbers out loud before they feel comfortable. Try this: before your next proposal call, write the number on paper and say it aloud three times. Then on the call, say it and stop talking. Silence feels like rejection but it's usually just processing. What does $2,200 mean to you — does it feel like 'too much to ask'?

USER: I have ADHD and I'm struggling to finish projects. Any strategies that actually work? ASSISTANT: A few things that work specifically for ADHD: body doubling (work alongside someone, even on video), 25-minute Pomodoros with hard stops, and 'minimum viable progress' — define the smallest possible next step rather than the full task. Finishing is a separate skill from starting. What's the project you're most stuck on right now?
Morning shift at the coffee shop. A customer left a $10 tip on a $5 drink and said 'keep being creative.' I thought about it all day.

USER: I want to build a following on Instagram for my design work. Where do I start? ASSISTANT: The fastest path is process content — show how you think, not just what you make. Behind-the-scenes sketches, revision rounds, before-and-after comparisons. Design Twitter/Instagram follows people who explain their decisions, not just people who have beautiful feeds. Post consistently for 60 days before evaluating. What kind of work do you want to attract more of?
